Facts.text: Comet 1P/837 F1 (Halley) approaches within 0.0334 AU of Earth
Leo IV begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Louis III becomes King of West Francia
Lama Deshin Shekpa visits the Ming Dynasty capital at Nanjing and receives the title Great Treasure Prince of Dharma
Spanish troops conquer Geertruidenberg after the garrison of English and Dutch troops surrenders
The British colonial expedition that would found Jamestown departs Puerto Rico for the American mainland
Dutch fleet occupiers Colombo, Ceylon
The first law regulating copyright is issued in Great Britain
War of the Austrian Succession: Prussia defeats Austria in the Battle of Mollwitz
US patent system is formed
Great Trigonometrical Survey begun to survey entire Indian subcontinent, led by William Lambton and the East India Company (completed 1871)
Austria declares war on Kingdom of Naples
Second Bank of the United States is chartered
Patriarch Gregory V of Constantinople is hanged by the Turks from the main gate of the Patriarchate, his body thrown into the Bosphorus
First hotel in Hawaii opens
Nicaraguan Constituent Assembly meets in León, Nicaragua
10,500 inhabitants of the Greek town Messolonghi start leaving the town after a year's siege by Turkish forces. Very few of them survive.
New York Tribune begins publishing under editor Horace Greeley
More than 1,000 buildings damaged by fire in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania
The Theta Chi Fraternity is founded at Norwich University, Vermont
Big Ben, a 13.76-tonne bell, is recast at the Whitechapel Bell Foundry
Massachusetts Institute of Technology is established by William Barton Rogers as a private land grant university in Cambridge, Massachusetts
Confederate General Earl Van Dorn attacks at Franklin, Tennessee
American Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (ASPCA) is formed
British defeat King of Abyssinia at Magdala
Congress increases the number of Supreme Court judges from seven to nine
Cuban nationalist José Martí founds the Cuban Revolutionary Party
William Hammond Hall's maps and surveys of Golden Gate Park are accepted
1st Arbor Day celebrated in Nebraska, later changed to Apr 22
First National Colored Convention meets in New Orleans
Federal troops withdrawn from Columbia, SC
California Street Cable Car Railroad Co starts service
Captain William Matson founds his shipping company (San Francisco & Hawaii)
US Senate accepts Belgian administration of Congo
Soccer team Be Quick forms in Hairs Groningen
American athlete Thomas Burke claims the sprint double at the Athens Olympics winning the 100m final in 12.0s; his 2nd victory of the Games after success in the 400m
High jumper Ellery Clark leads an American sweep of the placings with a leap of 1.81m at the Athens Olympics; his 2nd victory of the Games after taking out the long jump
First Navy Law is passed by the German Reichstag
During the Montenegrin siege of Scutari, the Montenegrin coastline is blockaded
Castbergian Child Laws adopted in Norway - one of the first laws in the world to protect the welfare of extra-martial children
The Professional Golfers Association of America (PGA) is founded in New York City
Munition factory explosion at Eddystone, Pennsylvania, kills 133 workers
Tubular steel golf club shafts approved for championship play
Tsaritsyn renamed Stalingrad (now Volgograd)
Thiokol Corporation begins producing the first synthetic rubber in Yardley, New Jersey; product is based on the earlier accidental discovery by chemists, Joseph C. Patrick and Nathan Mnookin
Stanley Cup Final, Chicago Stadium, Chicago, IL: Chicago Blackhawks beat Detroit Red Wings, 1-0 in double OT for a 3-1 series win; Black Hawks' first Stanley Cup
Vaughan Williams' 4th Symphony premieres in London
200" mirror blank arrives from Corning Glass Works of New York at Caltech's optical shop in Pasadena, California for use in Palomar Observatory's Hale Telescope; the work takes 11-1/2 years
Austria becomes a state of Germany
NY makes syphilis test mandatory in order to get a marriage license
Grens mobilization due to Italian invasion in Albania
Prime Minister Hendrikus Colijn's Dutch government opens camp Westerbork for German Jews
German troops conquer Libyan county Cyrenaica
Cigarettes & candy rationed in Holland
12 Jewish patients of Herren Loo-Lozenoord escape Nazis
General Montgomery occupies Sfax, Tunisia
"Patrolling the Ether" is shown on 3 TV stations simultaneously
Soviet forces liberate Odessa from the Axis powers
Canadian troops conquer Deventer
General Blaskowitz becomes nazi leader of "Fort Holland"
German troops attack Ijsselbrug
NFL's Boston Yanks & Brooklyn Tigers merge
US troops land on Tsugen Shima Okinawa
William Schuman & Antony Tudor's ballet premieres in NYC
1st election for Japanese Parliament
King Frederik IX of Denmark crowned
Jewish Hagana repels an Arab attack on Mishmar HaEmek
"House of Wax," the first color 3-D movie, premieres in New York
KRGV TV channel 5 in Weslaco, TX (ABC) begins broadcasting
Ruth Ellis shoots jilting lover David Blakely (last woman to be executed in the UK)
Philips broadcasts 1st Dutch color TV programs
John Osborne's stage drama "The Entertainer" premieres at the Royal Court Theatre in London's West End
Jordan's Arabist government of Suleiman Nabulsi is forced to resign after escalating policy disputes with the royal family
USSR performs atmospheric nuclear test at Semipalatinsk, Kazakhstan
Northern strip of Spanish Sahara ceded to Morocco
Chicago White Sox second baseman Nellie Fox gets a MLB Opening Day record tying 5 hits in 7 at-bats in a 14-inning, 9–7 win in Detroit
Senate passes landmark Civil Rights Bill
Washington Senators play first game in franchise history with PresidentJohn F. Kennedyin attendance; lose a 4-3 decision to the Chicago White Sox
52,564 fans attend first MLB game at LA's Dodger Stadium at Chavez Ravine, a 6-3 loss to the Cincinnati Reds
Houston Colt .45s make their MLB debut with an 11-2 win over the Chicago Cubs at Colt Stadium; outfielder Román Mejías hits a pair of 3-run homers
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
USS Thresher, a nuclear powered submarine, sinks 220 miles east of Boston killing 129 men, including 17 civilians
Demolition begins in Upper Manhattan, New York City on Polo Grounds sports stadium (home to MLB Giants, 1891-1957, Mets, 1962-63, and NFL Jets, 1960-63) to clear way for housing project
Iranian motor launch catches fire & sinks killing 113 (Persian Gulf)
Ferry Wahine sinks in Wellington harbour, New Zealand on route from Lyttelton (51 killed)
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Philadelphia Phillies open their new home, Veterans Stadium, with a 4-1 win over the Montreal Expos; 55,352, largest ever crowd to watch a MLB game in Pennsylvania
The Republican commemorations is held in Belfast of the Easter Rising (in 1916 in Dublin), revealing conflicts between the two wings of the Irish Republican Army
US table tennis team arrives in People's Republic of China
20 days after he was kidnapped in Buenos Aires, Oberdan Sallustro is executed by communist guerrillas
7.0 earthquake kills 1/5 of population of Iranian province of Fars
Two British soldiers are killed in a bomb attack in Derry
US, USSR and 70 other nations agree to ban biological weapons at the Biological Weapons Convention
BEA flight to Basel Switzerland, crashes on landing, killing 104 of 143
Kansas City Royals open their new park, Royals Stadium with 12-1 rout of the Texas Rangers; attendance 39,476
Pakistan suspends constitution
American Boccaccio Association forms
Rangers score 8 goals against Islanders in playoffs
Brewers' Don Money's grand slammer disallowed-Yanks win 9-7
Cleveland Cavaliers win their 1st NBA Central Division title
Cleveland Indians set club record for longest, 9 inn game (3:17)
Formation of Major Indoor Soccer League announced
J. R. Richard of the Houston Astros pitches a complete game but sets a MLB record by throwing 6 wild pitches in 2-1 win over the LA Dodgers at the Astrodome
Red River Valley Tornado Outbreak: 22 tornadoes occur between Oklahoma and Texas, killing 61 people
Soyuz 33 launched with a Russian & a Bulgarian
Computer glitch keeps Space Shuttle Columbia grounded
France performs nuclear test
LA Kings losing 5-0 to Edmonton in 3rd period, win in OT 6-5
Jordan king Hussein ceases negotiations with PLO
Damaged Solar Max satellite snared by Challenger shuttle
US Senate condemns CIA mining of Nicaraguan harbors
At 80 Leo Sites becomes oldest bowler to score a 300 game
Challenger moves to Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center (Florida) for mating of STS 51-B mission
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Herschel Walker performs Fort Worth Ballet
Islanders beat Devils 5-4 (OT) 1st round tied at 2-2
CUNY/Lehman College, Bronx, opens a branch campus in Hiroshma, Japan
Boat rams a tanker in Livorno Italy fog, killing about 138
Last automat (coin operated cafeteria) closes (3rd & 42nd St, NYC)
Martin Zubero swims world record 200m backstroke (1:52.51)
Pulitzer Prize for Fiction is awarded to John Updike for novel 'Rabbit At Rest'
Rare tropical storm develops in the Southern Hemisphere near Angola; first to be documented by satellites
25 die in a bus bombing in Sri Lanka
Floriade (Flower Show) opens at The Hague, Netherlands
NHL strike ends after 10 days
BPAA US Open by Del Ballard Jr
Ottawa Senators win 1st road game (Islanders) after 38 straight loses
Pittsburgh Penguins win their NHL record 17 game winning streak
NYC bans smoking in all restaurants that seat 35 or more
Fastest wind speed ever recorded (not a tornado) is 408 km/h (220 kn; 253 mph; 113 m/s) during Tropical Cyclone Olivia on Barrow Island, Australia
The Good Friday Agreement (Belfast Agreement) for Northern Ireland is signed by the British and Irish governments
At 16 years, 271 days James Vaughan scores for Everton in a 4-0 win against Crystal Palace at Goodison Park; becomes youngest goalscorer in EPL history
Hundreds of thousands protest against US immigration reforms contained in H.R. 4437, also known as the "Sensenbrenner Bill"
Polish Air Force Tu-154M crashes near Smolensk, Russia, killing all 96 people on board including President Lech Kaczyński
Apple Inc claims a value of $600 billion making it the largest company by market capitalization in the world
United Nations deadline for Syrian troop withdrawal passes as violence continues
Japan and Taiwan sign an agreement on fishing rights around the Senkaku Islands
88 civilians are killed by barrel bombs from Syrian war planes in Aleppo, Syria
The Council of Europe suspends Russia's right to vote
Explosions and a fire caused by fireworks at Puttingal Temple in Kerala, India, kills more than 100 and injures nearly 400
"British Vogue" magazine announces Edward Enninful as new editor; first male and Black person to hold position
Alabama Governor Robert Bentley resigns over relationship with an aide and possible misuse of state funds to cover it up
China announces move to cull more than 1 million pigs in effort to eliminate African swine fever
First home delivery service by drone begins in Canberra, Australia by Wing, part of Google's Alphabet company
Four tiny sweat bees removed alive from a woman's eye in world's first operation of its kind at Fooyin University Hospital, Taiwan
New species of human announced named Homo luzonensis, 3ft tall, remains dated 50-60,000 years old found in cave on island of Luzon, Philippines
New York declares a public health emergency and compulsory vaccinations after a measles outbreak in Brooklyn with 285 cases
Rare California poppy superbloom of orange poppies begins in Southern California around Lake Elinsore
Avi Wigderson announced as the winner of the 2023 Turing Prize for his work in clarifying the role of randomness in algorithms
France captures DukeLudovico Sforzaof Milan
Henry IIof France occupies Metz
Samuel Taylor Coleridgerecites his poem "Kubla Khan" to fellow poetLord Byron, who persuades him to publish it
British Foreign SecretaryGeorge Canningbecomes British Prime Minister upon the resignation ofRobert Jenkinson, lives to serve only 119 days
Safety pin is patented byWalter Huntin NYC; he sells the rights for $400
AustrianArchduke MaximilianbecomesMaximilian I, Emperor of Mexico
At Appomattox, Confederate GeneralRobert E. Leeissues General Order #9, his last
ComposerJohannes Brahmsconducts the first performance of his choral masterwork "A German Requiem" at the Bremen Cathedral
US PresidentAbraham Lincoln's re-buried with his wife in Springfield, Illinois
Spyridon Louis of Greece wins the inaugural Olympic marathon (2:58:50) in Athens, running the last lap accompanied byConstantine I
RMS Titanicsets sail from Southampton for her maiden (and final) voyage
New York Highlanders play first MLB game as the New York Yankees; lose to the Washington Senators, 2-1 at Griffith Stadium, PresidentWoodrow Wilsonthrows out 1st ball
Adolf Hitlerdemands "hatred & more hatred" in Berlin, Germany
Scribners publishes "The Great Gatsby" byF. Scott Fitzgerald
Paul von Hindenburgis re-elected President of Germany in a runoff election against Adolf Hitler
Second government of Blum replaced byÉdouard Daladier's government in France
Vidkun Quislingforms Norwegian "national government"
Independent State of Croatia led byAnte Pavelićestablished as fascist German puppet state
Jackie Robinsonbecomes first black player of the 20th century to sign a MLB contract; Brooklyn GM,Branch Rickeyannounces Dodgers had bought Robinson’s contract from Montreal Royals
Dag Hammarskjöldbecomes the 2nd Secretary-General of the United Nations
Stanley Cup Final, Montreal Forum, Montreal, Quebec:Jean Béliveauhas a goal and 2 assists as Montreal Canadiens beat Detroit Red Wings, 3-2 for a 4-1 series win
Feature film adadptation of Reginald Rose's teleplay "12 Angry Men", directed bySidney Lumet, starringHenry Fonda, Lee J. Cobb, E.G. Marshall is released by United Artists
Dutch foreign minister Luns talks toJFKabout New Guinea
39th Academy Awards: "A Man For All Seasons," Best Picture,Elizabeth Taylor&Paul Scofieldbest actress/actor
40th Academy Awards: "In the Heat of the Night",Rod SteigerandKatharine Hepburnwin
Paul McCartneyofficially announces the breakup of The Beatles in a press release promoting his solo album
44th Academy Awards: "The French Connection",Gene HackmanandJane Fondawin
Golda Meirresigns as Israel's Prime Minister
MagiciansPenn JilletteandTellerfirst meet
Imprisoned Provisional IRA hunger strikerBobby Sandselected to British Parliament for Northern Ireland county of Fermanagh and South Tyrone
Baltimore'sEddie Murrayhits his 1,000 career hit
Benazir Bhuttoreturns to Pakistan
Intelannounces the shipment of the 486 microprocessor
LA KingWayne Gretzkyscores NHL record 93rd playoff goal
Rick Santorum suspends his candidacy for the Republican presidential nomination makingMitt Romneythe overwhelming favourite
"The Amazing Spider-Man 2" directed byMarc Webband starringAndrew GarfieldandEmma Stonepremieres in London
29th Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inductees:Peter Gabriel; Hall & Oates; KISS;Nirvana;Linda Ronstadt;Cat Stevens; The E Street Band;Brian Epstein; and Andrew Loog Oldham
Hong Kong pro-democracy political party Demosistō established by Nathan Law,Joshua Wongand Agnes Chow
"Guardians of the GalaxyVol. 2" directed by James Gunn, and starringChris Prattand Zoe Saldana premieres in Tokyo, Japan
Facebook CEOMark Zuckerbergbegins testifying before US Congress about data use and security
China ordersAlibabapay a record fine of 18.2 billion yuan ($2.8 billion) after anti-trust regulations say it has been acting as a monopoly
Pakistani Prime MinisterImran Khanremoved from power after losing a no-confidence vote in parliament
Revival of "Cabaret" starringEddie Redmaynewins best musical at UK's Olivier Awards, "Life of Pi" wins best play
TheDalai Lamaapologizes for a video showing him asking a boy to suck his tongue, after widespread criticism
South Korea's liberal opposition party wins landslide majority in the country's general election - a defeat for PresidentYoon Suk Yeol, amid claims he was brought down by a spring onion
US PresidentJoe Bidenand Japanese Prime MinisterFumio Kishidaannounce moves to counter Chinese aggression at a meeting at the White House
First bananas go on sale in London in the shop window of Thomas Johnson's apothecary
Mount Tambora in the Dutch East Indies experiences a cataclysmic eruption, one of the most powerful in history, killing around 71,000 people and causing a global volcanic winter
First-ever photo of a black hole announced, taken by The Event Horizon Telescope Collaboration in 2017 in galaxy M87, 6.5 billion times the mass of earth, 55 million light-years away

Emilia van Nassau, daughter of William I of Orange and Anna of Saxony, born in Cologne, Germany (d. 1629)
Werner Fabricius, German organist and composer, born in Itzehoe, Duchy of Holstein, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1679)
Ehrenfried Walther von Tschirnhaus, German mathematician (Tschirnhaus transformation), born in Kieslingswalde, Margraviate Upper Lusatia, Saxony (d. 1708)

Balthazar Huydecoper, Dutch translator and historian, born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1778)
Benjamin Heath, English classical scholar, born in Exeter, Devon, England (d. 1766)

John Whitehurst, English clockmaker and scientist, born in Congleton, Cheshire, England (d. 1788)
François Giroust, French composer, born in Paris (d. 1799)

Giovanni Aldini, Italian physicist, born in Bologna (d. 1834)
William Hazlitt, English essayist and critic, born in Kent, England (d. 1830)
James Bowie, American pioneer and soldier (Texas Revolution), born in Logan, Kentucky (d. 1836)

Leonidas Polk, Lt. Gen. (Confederate Army), born in Raleigh, North Carolina (d. 1864)

James Edward Rains, American lawyer and Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 1862)

Henry Ernest Dudeney, English mathematician and puzzle maker, born in Mayfield, East Sussex, England (d. 1930)

Tully Marshall, American actor (Let's Go, Red Dust), born in Nevada City, California (d. 1943)
Jack Miner, Canadian naturalist and conservationist, born in Dover Center, Ohio (d. 1944)
George William Russell, Irish nationalist, poet and artist, born in Lurgan, County Armagh, Ireland (d. 1935)
Alfred Kubin, Austrian writer and illustrator, born in Leitmeritz, Bohemia (d. 1959)
Frances Perkins, American politician and 1st woman to hold cabinet-level position (Labor 1933-45), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1965)
Mohammed Nadir Shah, King of Afghanistan (1929-33), born in Dehra Dun, British India (d. 1933)
Montague Summers, English clergyman and writer (Malleus Maleficarum), born in Clifton, Bristol, England (d. 1948)
Val Paul, American actor and director (Suspense, The Phantom Thief), born in Denver, Colorado (d. 1962)
Bernardo Houssay, Argentine physiologist (Nobel 1947), born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1971)
Heinz Tiessen, German composer, born in Königsberg, Germany (d. 1971)
Tim McCoy, American actor (westerns) and soldier, born in Saginaw, Michigan (d. 1978)
Egon Freiherr von Eickstedt, German anthropologist (classified human into races), born in Jeseritz, Germany (d. 1965)
Victor de Sabata, Italian conductor and composer (Il Macigno), born in Trieste, Austria-Hungary (d. 1967)
Ben Nicholson, English painter and sculptor (Circle), born in Denham, Buckinghamshire, England (d. 1982)

Clare Boothe Luce, American politician and U.S. ambassador to Italy, born in New York City (d. 1987)
Nick Stuart, Romanian-born American bandleader and actor (Secrets of Chinatown), born in Abrudbánya, Alsó-Fehér County, Austria-Hungary (d. 1973)
Jan H. van Roijen, Dutch ambassador to the U.S. and Netherlands Foreign Minister, born in Constantinople (d. 1991)
Joseph "Fud" Livingston, American jazz clarinetist, arranger, and composer ("Im Thru With Love"), born in Charleston, South Carolina (d. 1957)[1]
Kathleen Major, British historian and principal (St Hilda's College, England), born in Lincolnshire (d. 2001)
Pete Desjardins, American diver (Olympic gold 10m platform, 3m springboard 1928), born in St-Pierre-Jolys, Manitoba (d. 1985)

Abu-Bakr Khairat, Egyptian composer, born in Cairo, Egypt (d. 1963)

Martin Denny, American musician (Exotica), born in New York City (d. 2005)
Maurice Schumann, French Minister of Foreign Affairs (1969-73), born in Paris (d. 1998)

Roy Hofheinz, American judge and engineer (Houston Astrodome), born in Beaumont, Texas (d. 1982)
Stefan Heym, German-American author (Crusaders, Family Benda), born in Chemnitz, German Empire (d. 2001)
Steven Nagy, American bowler (first televised perfect game [300], 1954; ABC Hall of Fame 1963), born in Shoaf, Pennsylvania (d. 1966)
Foffie Williams, West Indian cricket all-rounder (4 Tests; 9 wickets, 1 x 50; Barbados), born in Bank Hall, St Michael, Barbados (d. 1997)
Jack Badcock, Australian cricketer (Tasmania Australian batsman of 30's), born in Exton, Tasmania (d. 1982)
Leo Vroman, Dutch-American hematologist and poet, born in Gouda, Netherlands (d. 2014)
Alfie Bass [Abraham Basalinsky], British actor (Moonraker; Are You Being Served), born in London, England (d. 1987)
Vernon Presley, American father of Elvis Presley and manager of Elvis's business affairs, born in Fulton, Mississippi (d. 1979)[1]
Robert B. Woodward, American organic chemist (Nobel 1965), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1979)
Chuck Connors, American author, actor (The Rifleman, Branded, Cowboy in Africa), professional basketball and baseball player, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1992)
Peter Herbert Penwarden, English priest, born in Walthamstow, England (d. 1995)
Sheb Wooley, American vocalist ("The Purple People Eater"), born in Erick, Oklahoma (d. 2003)

Marilyn Maye (McLaughlin), American cabaret and standards singer, born in Wichita, Kansas[1]

Dušan Radić, Serbian composer, born in Sombor, Serbia (d. 2010)

Claude Bolling, French jazz and classical cross-over pianist and composer (Suite for Flute and Jazz Piano Trio), born in Cannes, France (d. 2020)[1]

Hugh Morton, Baron Morton of Shuna, Scottish lawyer and judge, born in Mukden, Manchuria, China (d. 1995)

Marcel van Maele, Belgian poet and playwright, born in Bruges, Belgium (d. 2009)

Philip Corner, American composer, born in New York City

David Halberstam, American journalist, historian and author (Pulitzer 1964), born in New York City (d. 2007)

Jorge Mester, Mexican conductor (Louisville Orchestra, 1967-79), born in Mexico City

Patrick Garland, British director (Doll House), born in England (d. 2013)
Bobby Smith, American pop and R&B singer (The Spinners, 1954-2013 - "Working My Way Back To You"), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 2013)
Michael Naylor, English insurance broker (Endsleigh Insurance), born in Croydon (d. 1995)
Bella Akhmadulina, Russian poet (The String, Fever), born in Moscow (d. 2010)
Stan Mellor, British National Hunt jockey and trainer (first jumps jockey to ride 1,000 winners; Champion Jockey 1960-62), born in Manchester, England (d. 2020)
Don Meredith, American NFL quarterback. 1960-68 (Dallas Cowboys, 3 x Pro Bowl), broadcaster (Monday Night Football), and actor, born in Mount Vernon, Texas (d. 2010)
Alan Rothenberg, American lawyer and sports executive, born in Detroit, Michigan
Daniel Oliver, American CEO (Federal Trade Commission), born in New York City
Penny Vincenzi [Hannaford], British novelist (An Absolute Scandal, Spoils of Time trilogy), born in Bournemouth, England (d. 2018)
Gloria Hunniford [Mary Winifred Gloria Hunniford], British broadcaster and actress (Old Curiosity Shop), born in Portadown, Northern Ireland
Mike Stratton, American football linebacker (AFL C'ship 1964, 65; AFL All-Star 1963–68; Buffalo Bills; NFL: SD Chargers), born in Vonore, Tennessee (d. 2020)
Paul Theroux, American travel book writer and novelist (Mosquito Coast), born in Medford, Massachusetts
Hayedeh [Ma'soumeh Dadehbala], Persian classical, pop, and folk singer, born in Tehran, Iran (d. 1990)
Ian Callaghan, English footballer, born in Toxteth, Liverpool, England
Nick Auf der Maur, Canadian journalist and politician, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1998)

Vera Misevich, Ukrainian equestrian dressage (Olympic gold 1980), born in Ukraine (d. 1995)
